coredata.dc:title: Phenylboronic acid modified carbon dots for improved protein delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Shan
Xie, Zhigang
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: It is a great challenge to establish a universal nanocarrier for delivering different proteins, due to the differences of proteins in molecular weights, isoelectric points and spatial structures. Even though some carriers including polymer, liposomes and inorganic nanomaterials have been utilized to deliver proteins, most of them are only suitable for specific proteins. In the present work, amine-riched carbon dots (CDs) were introduced phenyl boronic acid to form CD-B, which could load various proteins with considerable contents. Hemoglobin (Hb) and glucose oxidase (GOX) were used as models to form Hb@CD-B and GOX@CD-B nanohybrids, and their loading content and efficiency were improved significantly. Most importantly, these protein hybrids could be internalized by the living cells, and the protein activities were maintained effectively. This work developed a versatile platform for efficacious loading proteins with compromising activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Protein delivery
Nanocarrier
Noncovalent interactions
Carbon dots
Phenylboronic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of phenylboronic acid modified CDs (CD-B)
Gas-binding capacity of Hb in Hb@CD-B
Determination of glucose oxidase activity in GOX@CD-B
The ability of GOX@CD-B to produce H2O2
Synthesis and characterization of CDs and CD-B

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, d) TEM images of CDs and CD-B. The images inserted in Fig. 1a and 1d are the size distributions of CDs and CD-B, respectively. (b, e) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (λex = 300–440 nm) and CD-B (λex = 300–440 nm). (c, f) UV − Vis absorption and excitation spectra of CDs and CD-B.
(a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) Full scan and (c) C1s (d) N1s, (e) O1s, and (f) B1s HR XPS spectra of CD-B.

(a) TEM image of Hb@CD-B. (b) Particle size of Hb@CD-B determined by DLS. (c) Zeta-potentials of CD-B, Hb and Hb@CD-B. (d) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CD-B and Hb@CD-B. Inset are the photoes of CD-B and Hb@CD-B. (e) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Hb@CD-B in different gas-binding states (CO, O2 or air). Inset is an enlarged UV–Vis curve ranging from 500 to 600 nm. (f) SDS-PAGE analysis of Hb and Hb@CD-B (1: marker; 2: free Hb; 3: Hb@CD-B). (g) CLSM images of HeLa cells incubated at 37 °C for 4 h (CDs: 5 μg/mL, CD-B: 5 μg/mL, Hb: 5 μg/mL). (488 nm: the green channel; 555 nm: the red channel). Scale bar, 40 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) TEM, (b) DLS analysis and (c) Zeta-potential of GOX@CD-B. (d) Time-dependent absorption spectra of O-dianisidine at 460 nm after free GOX and GOX@CD-B cascade reaction. Insets are the photos of the working solutions before and after GOX and GOX@CD-B treatment. (e) Changes of enzymatic activity of GOX and GOX@CD-B stored at room temperature. (f) SDS-PAGE analysis of free GOX and GOX@CD-B (1: marker; 2: GOX; 3: GOX@CD-B). (g) UV − vis absorption spectra of formed Ti(IV)O2SO4 at different H2O2 concentration. (h) The standard curve of H2O2 concentration derived from the of Ti(IV)O2SO4 at Abs 407 nm. (i) The amount of H2O2 produced by native GOX and GOX@CD-B, at the same concentration of GOX (50 μg/mL).

(a) Cell viabilities of HeLa cells treated with CD-B, GOX and CD-B@GOX. (b) The ROS production in HeLa cells after treated with DMEM, GOX@CD-B, GOX (GOX: 2 µg/mL), respectively. (c) Fluorescence images of PI (red, dead cells) and calcein AM (green, live cells) co-stained HeLa cells with different treatments, scale bar is 100 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The fabrication of Hb@CD-B and GOX@CD-B nanocomposites for intracellular protein delivery.

head.ce:title: Phenylboronic acid modified carbon dots for improved protein delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Polymer Physics and Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is a great challenge to establish a universal nanocarrier for delivering different proteins, due to the differences of proteins in molecular weights, isoelectric points and spatial structures. Even though some carriers including polymer, liposomes and inorganic nanomaterials have been utilized to deliver proteins, most of them are only suitable for specific proteins. In the present work, amine-riched carbon dots (CDs) were introduced phenyl boronic acid to form CD-B, which could load various proteins with considerable contents. Hemoglobin (Hb) and glucose oxidase (GOX) were used as models to form Hb@CD-B and GOX@CD-B nanohybrids, and their loading content and efficiency were improved significantly. Most importantly, these protein hybrids could be internalized by the living cells, and the protein activities were maintained effectively. This work developed a versatile platform for efficacious loading proteins with compromising activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Proteins are vital in life activities, such as enzyme catalysis, signal transduction, and regulation of gene transcription etc. (Zelikin et al., 2016; Mitragotri et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2016; Bizeau and Mertz, 2021). In the past decades, protein drugs have played an important role in biomedicine, for example the monoclonal antibodies and immunologic agents PD-1 et al. (Hu et al., 2018). Compared with small molecule drugs, protein drugs possess many advantages, including strong specificity, low side effects, short clinical trial cycle (Wang et al., 2017; Nagarani et al., 2018; Leader et al., 2008). However, due to the large molecular weight, hydrophilicity and low negative charge density of proteins, it is difficult for proteins to cross cell membranes (Yim et al., 2016; Postupalenko et al., 2015). Therefore, it is very important to develop a safe and efficient protein intracellular delivery vector for the research of biomedicine and life sciences.

Since proteins have different molecular weights, isoelectric points and spatial structures (Mitragotri et al., 2014; Gu et al., 2011), it is a great challenge to build a universal nanodelivery platform that is suitable for a multitude of proteins. At present, although some nanocarriers such as polymers (Lv et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2018b; Lee et al., 2017; Xiao et al., 2017; Ng et al., 2014; Lyu et al., 2019; Lv et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020), liposomes (Zhang et al., 2018a; Schulze et al., 2019; Zuris et al., 2015) and metal nanoparticles (Zhang and Cheng, 2020; Ding et al., 2014) have be utilized to deliver proteins, they generally lack universal binding sites and are only suitable for intracellular delivery of specific proteins. In order to obtain a universal protein carrier, it is necessary to chemically modify the protein by introducing a large number of negative groups (Lee et al., 2009; Lee et al., 2010), anionic polymers (Postupalenko et al., 2014; Lamson et al., 2020), nucleic acids(Brodin et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2015) or other functional groups into the protein. These chemical modifications not only involve complicated chemical or biological synthesis, but also encounter some problems, for example, the decrease of biological activity of protein and low loading rate. How to design a simple intracellular protein delivery system without chemical modification is a key technology to be solved urgently. The ideal protein intracellular delivery vector can form a stable complex with protein without reducing the biological activity of protein, and has high protein loading and efficiency.

Carbon dots (CDs) possess the characteristics of low cost, small size, large surface area, easy surface functionalization, strong hydrophilicity and good biocompatibility, which provide unique advantages for them to act as vectors (Sun et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2020b; Hua et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2020a; Wang et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2020a; Su et al., 2020). Our group have constructed a series of nanohybrids by the co-assembly of CDs with anticancer drugs (Sun et al., 2017), organic dyes (Zhang et al., 2018c; Su et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2020b) and proteins (Zhang et al., 2016; Li et al., 2018). Especially, we have constructed CDs-green fluorescent protein (EGFP) nanocomposite for efficient intracellular EGFP delivery (Zhang et al., 2016). We also fabricated CDs-ricin B chain (RTB) (Li et al., 2018) nanohybrids for promoting macrophage activation. The results demonstrate that CDs might be ideal intracellular delivery vectors for proteins. Nevertheless, constructing a universal CDs-based protein delivery system remains a huge challenge (Liu et al., 2019).

Herein, amino-riched CDs were synthesized from polyethylene imine (PEI) and glyoxal via solvothermal method. Then CDs were decorated with 4-(bromomethyl)phenylboronicacid to form CD-B. On the one hand, the phenylboronic acid (PA) groups on CD-B can coordinate with proteins via B-N complexation and cation-π interactions (Liu and He, 2017; Chen et al., 2016; Lv et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2013). On the other hand, the amine groups on CDs can bind with anionic groups on proteins. Therefore, CD-B might have high binding affinity with different types of proteins. In order to demonstrate our hypothesis, we loaded two types of proteins hemoglobin (Hb) and glucose oxidase (GOX) onto CD-B and fabricated Hb@CD-B and GOX@CD-B nanocomposites, respectively (Scheme 1 ). The encapsulation efficiency of Hb in Hb@CD-B and Hb@CDs, GOX in GOX@CD-B and GOX@CDs is 94.3% and 74.3%, 62.3% and 2.0%, respectively, indicating that CD-B can significantly enhance the protein loading efficiency. In addition, both Hb@CD-B and GOX@CD-B are stable and can realize protein intracellular delivery without lowering the biological activity of proteins.

Polyethylene imine (Mw 1800), glyoxal, glucose and 4-(bromomethyl) phenylboronic acid were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Company. Bovine hemoglobin (Hb, 64.5 KDa), 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-Diphenyltetra-zolium bromide (MTT) and Horseradish peroxidase (HRP) were purchased from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Glucose oxidase (GOX, 144.46 KDa) and O-dianisidine (98%) were purchased from Saen Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Reactive oxygen ROS analysis kit and living dead cell staining kit were purchased from Jiangsu KeyGEN Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water was obtained from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA). All chemicals were obtained from commercial sources and used without further purification.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained on a JEOL JEM–1011 (Japan) at the accelerating voltage of 100 kV. Fluorescence emission spectra were carried out on a LS-55 fluorophotometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were conducted on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of CD-B was recorded on a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer from 4000 to 500 cm−1. X-Ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were carried out on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 Multitechnique Surface Analysis. Zeta potential was determined by using a Malvern Zeta-sizer Nano. MTT assays were measured at 490 nm by a microplate reader (BioTek, EXL808). Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images were taken using a Zeiss LSM 700 (Zurich, Switzerland).

The ethanol solution (10 mL) of polyethylene imine (PEI, 0.18 g) and glyoxal (4.6 μL) was transferred to 25 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 2 h. After cooled to room temperature, the solution was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 6 h, CDs was obtained by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of phenylboronic acid modified CDs (CD-B)
ce:section.ce:para: The mixture of CDs (10 mg) and 4-(bromomethyl) phenylboronicacid (10 mg) were dissolved in methanol (5 mL) and stirred for 24 h, Then the crude product was dialyzed against water for 24 h, and CD-B was obtained by freeze-drying.

The solution of CD-B (2 mg/mL, 1 mL) and Hb (2 mg/mL, 1 mL) were mixed and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was then centrifuged in an ultrafiltration tube for 5 min (3500 rpm/min) to remove native Hb. Finally, the samples were dispersed in solution and stored in the refrigerator (4 °C). GOX@CD-B was obtained by the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas-binding capacity of Hb in Hb@CD-B
ce:section.ce:para: The gas-binding capacity of Hb under different gases (CO, air) could be monitored by UV–vis spectrophotometry. First, a small amount of ascorbic acid was added to 3 mL Hb@CD-B solution (Hb: 100 μg/mL), CO gas was then injected into Hb@CD-B solution for 10 min, carbon oxyhemoglobin conjugate (COHb) was obtained. Then, a certain amount of oxygen was injected into the COHb solution and converted into an oxygenated hemoglobin conjugate (OxyHb). The binding process of Hb with different atmospheres were detected by UV spectrum, and the pure hemoglobin was used as control group.

Human cervix carcinoma cells (Hela cells) were seeded in 6-well plates (5 × 104 cells per well) incubate for 24 h in the 37 °C constant temperature incubator. Then same concentrations of CDs, Hb@CDs, CD-B and Hb@CD-B (CDs: 5 μg/mL, CD-B: 5 μg/mL, Hb: 5 μg/mL) were incubated with the cells for 4 h. Later, old media was removed, cells were washed three times with PBS, and cells were fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min, and syringed with PBS again. Finally, cells were observed and photographed by CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of glucose oxidase activity in GOX@CD-B
ce:section.ce:para: The activity of GOX was assessed primarily by its cascade reaction with horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and O-dianisidine. The evaluation method is mainly through GOX catalytic oxidation glucose of working fluid to produce H2O2, the resulting H2O2 are then oxidized O-dianisidine to form an oxidation state of O-dianisidine (oxidized O-dianisidine) under the catalysis of peroxidase, and oxidized O-dianisidine has UV characteristic absorption peak at 460 nm(Li et al., 2007).

At the beginning of the test, we added GOX@CD-B or free GOX solution (0.1 mL) to the detection working liquid (free GOX as the control group). Then we measured the kinetic curve of this continuous catalytic reaction over time via monitoring the absorption intensity at 460 nm by UV spectrometer, moreover, the color change of the detection working fluid was clearly observed with the naked eyes. Activity of GOX was represented by the initial slope of the reaction kinetics curve.

Detection working liquid consists of three parts, 0.33 mM O-dianisidine solution (2.5 mL), 18% glucose solution (0.3 mL), and 0.02% HRP (0.1 mL), respectively. All of solutions were prepared with 0.1 M PBS buffer solution (pH 6.0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ability of GOX@CD-B to produce H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: To futher test the ability of GOX in GOX@CD-B to produce H2O2, we evaluated it with Titanium (IV) oxysulfate (Ti (IV)OSO4). The detection method is based on H2O2 oxidation Ti (IV) OSO4 to form yellow peroxide complex Ti(IV)O2SO4, which has UV characteristic absorption peak at 407 nm (Fan et al., 2017).

GOX@CD-B dispersion or GOX solution was incubated with glucose solution (100 μg/mL, 10 mL) in a 37 °C constant temperature bath. 100 μL of solution was removed at different time points and mixed with 500 μL of acetone, then 50 μL Ti (IV) OSO4 solution (30 mM), 100 μL NH3·H2O and 500 μL 1 M H2SO4 were added. The absorbance at 407 nm was measured and the concentration of H2O2 was determined according to the fitted standard curve.

We characterized successful loading of Hb and GOX by SDS-PAGE gel electrophoresis, the specific process with reference to the literature (Li et al., 2011a). First; we prepared 12% electrophoresis separation adhesive (lower layer) and 5% enrichment adhesive (upper layer). Second, various solutions containing proteins (Hb, Hb@CD-B, GOX, GOX@CD-B) were mixed with SDS, β- mercaptoethanol and bromophenol blue, and heated to 100 °C for 10 min to make protein denaturation. The above samples were added to the upper layer in turn, and electrophoresis was carried out at 80 mV for 40 min, and the voltage adjusted to 180 mV for 1 h. After electrophoresis, the lower layer was removed and dyed with Coomassie Brilliant Blue for 0.5 h. Finally, the lower layer was decolored in a decolorizing solution several times until the strips was clear.

Human cervix carcinoma cells (Hela cells) were seeded in 6-well plates (5 × 104 cells per well) incubate for 24 h in the 37 °C constant temperature incubator. Then different concentrations of GOX@CD-B (0.25, 0.5 and 1.0 μg/mL) were incubated with the cells for 0.5 h. Later, GOX@CD-B was removed, cells were washed three times with PBS, and cells were fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min, and syringed with PBS again. Finally, cells were observed and photographed by CLSM.

We used commercial reactive oxygen testing kit to detect H2O2 production in cells. HeLa cells were first planted into 6-well culture plates with a sterile cover slide. After 24 h, the normal medium was removed and the cells were thoroughly cleaned three times with PBS, the medium containing GOX and GOX@CD-B (GOX:2 μg/mL) were added into 6-well culture plates and continued to be cultured at 37 °C for 2 h. Then washed the cells three times with PBS, and the reactive oxygen species test agent was added to each pore and incubated for 30 min. After that, the cells were washed three times with the FBS-free medium and twice with PBS. Cells do not need to be fixed, directly made into slices by 50% glycerol and rapidly observed with CLSM.

First of all, we evaluated the cytotoxicity of CDs by using MTT assay. Hela cells were seeded in 96-well plates at an initial density of 2 × 104 cells per well and incubated in 100 μL of DMEM at 37 °C in 5% CO2 atmosphere for overnight. Different concentrations of CDs solution was used to supersede the original medium and incubated with cells for 24 h. After that, each well was treated with 20 μL of MTT solution for 4 h. After careful removal of the culture medium supernatant, 150 μL of DMSO was added to each well to dissolve the formed violet formazan crystals. Finally, the plates were shaken for 5 min, and the absorbance of violet product was quantified at 490 nm by a microplate reader.
The cytotoxicities of CD-B, GOX and CD-B@GOX were assessed in the similar way.

HeLa cells were stained with the calcein-AM/propidium iodide (PI) to identify dead (red) and live (green) cells. Hela cells were seeded in 6-well plates at an initial density of 1 × 105 cells per well and incubated in 1.5 mL of DMEM at 37 °C in 5% CO2 atmosphere for overnight. Hela cells were incubated with different concentrations of CD-B, GOX or GOX@CD-B for 24 h. After the medium was discarded, 1 mL of Calcein-AM/PI mixture was added and incubated with cells for 30 min (avoid light). Then the staining solution was aspirated and PBS (1 mL) was added to each well. Eventually, fluorescence images were acquired via fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and CD-B
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared from PEI and glyoxal via solvothermal method. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CDs (Fig. 1 a) indicates that CDs are spherical nanoparticles with the average diameter of 11.4 nm. CDs possess excitation-dependent emission with the maximum at 468 nm under 340 nm excitation (Fig. 1b). One characteristic peak at 365 nm appears in the absorption spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1c, black solid line), which is attributed to the n-π* transition of CO and CN. Moreover, one maximum excitation band at 340 nm was observed in the excitation spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1c, blue solid line), which is consistent with the result concluded from emission spectrum.

CD-B was synthesized through the reaction of CDs with 4-(bromomethyl)phenylboronic acid at a weight ratio of 1:1. CD-B are also well-dispersed nanoparticles (Fig. 1d) and its average size is 22.5 nm. The excitation-dependent emission spectra of CD-B have a maximum at 470 nm, which is consistent with CDs fluorescence (Fig. 1e). UV–vis spectrum of CD-B exhibits an absorption peak at 340 nm, and its maximum excitation band exits in 340 nm (Fig. 1f). The above results show that the CD-B retains the optical properties of CDs.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 2 a) proves that there are a number of functional groups in CD-B: OH (3276 cm−1), CH (2842–3024 cm−1), CO (1758 cm−1), CC (1562 cm−1), CN (1486 cm−1), CO (1185 cm−1). Besides, there are BO (1395 cm−1) and BC (1042 cm−1) on the surface of CD-B. Furthermore, the element composition of CD-B was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique. Fig. 2b shows the full-scan survey of CD-B. Four main peaks at 284, 400, 533 and 191.1 eV correspond to C1s, N 1s, O1s, and B1s, respectively. The C1s high-resolution (HR) XPS spectrum (Fig. 2c) consists of three peaks, which correspond to CC (284.2 eV), CN (285.3 eV), CO (288.3 eV), respectively. Fig. 2d displays the N 1s HR XPS spectrum of CD-B, it confirms the presence of CN (400.6 eV) and NH (398.8 eV). The O1s HR spectrum also shows two peaks, which reveals the presence of CO (532.3 eV) and CO (534.1 eV) (Fig. 2e). The B1s HR XPS spectrum shows two peaks at 187.4 eV (BC) and 191.10 eV (BO), respectively (Fig. 2f).

In order to evaluate the delivery capability of CD-B, Hb was selected as a model protein to assemble with CD-B and construct Hb@CD-B hybrids via simple noncovalent interactions. TEM shows Hb@CD-B have spherical morphology with size raging from 132 nm to 195 nm (Fig. 3 a). The particle size of Hb@CD-B measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. 3b) is 221 nm, which is larger than that determined by TEM. Because the size determined by DLS represents the hydrodynamic diameter, whereas TEM characterizes the samples in the solid state, shrinkage will occur while the Hb@CD-B is dried. Zeta potential of CD-B, Hb and Hb@CD-B (Fig. 3c) is 56.2, −26.1 and 24.4 mV, respectively, proves the successful fabrication of Hb@CD-B. As shown in Fig. 3d, one characteristic peak corresponding to Hb appears at 405 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Hb@CD-B, accompanied by the solution changing from colorless to light brown, further confirming that Hb was loaded onto CD-B definitely. According to standard curve (Fig. S1), the encapsulation efficiency of Hb in Hb@CD-B is calculated to be 94.3%. which is higher than that of Hb@CDs (74.3%), verifying that the conjugation of PA with CDs can improve protein delivery. The stabilities of Hb, Hb@CDs and Hb@CD-B solution at the same Hb concentration were evaluated and shown in Fig. S2a. On the first day, all of them were transparent. However, on the second day, a lot of precipitation was observed in Hb and Hb@CDs solution. By contrast, only little aggregation appeared in Hb@CD-B, indicating that CD-B can maintain the stability of Hb.

Hb has distinctive absorption band in different binding states (Li et al., 2011b). Because Hb is easily oxidized to methemoglobin (met-Hb) in air, Hb@CD-B prepared in this paper is in oxidation state, the solution is brown and the maximum absorption peak is at 405 nm. After adding the reducing agent ascorbic acid, methemoglobin was reduced to ferrous hemoglobin. As shown in Fig. 3e, When Hb@CD-B is subjected to CO, the peak is redshifted to 418 nm along with two typical peaks at 537 and 575 nm, which are characteristic absorption bands of carboxyhemoglobin (COHb). Whereas the typical peaks of oxyhemoglobin (OxyHb) appear at 412, 538 and 572 nm under air atmosphere. Hb is able to complex with various gas ligands under different atmospheres (Fig. S2b). The combined state of different gases can be converted reversibly. These results indicate that Hb@CD-B retains the ability of native Hb to bind and release oxygen.

We used sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis to analyze Hb@CD-B. The results for native Hb and Hb@CD-B are shown in Fig. 3f. For free Hb and the digested Hb@CD-B, a band corresponding to the molecular weight of 25 KDa monomeric Hb was observed on the gel, which further certifying the triumphant complexation of Hb with CD-B. The CLSM images shows bright red fluorescence and green fluorescence, indicating that hemoglobin was successfully delivered into the HeLa cell (Fig. 3g).

In order to validate the universality of CD-B for protein delivery, GOX was selected the other model protein to fabricate corresponding protein-CD-B nanocomposite (GOX@CD-B). TEM image (Fig. 4 a) displays that GOX@CD-B is monodisperse with the diameter of 147 nm. The size distribution of GOX@CD-B determined by DLS (Fig. 4b) is 166 nm. The zeta potential of GOX@CD-B (Fig. 4c) is 31.5 mV, which is more negative than CD-B (56.2 mV), while more positive than GOX (−20.2 mV). The results certify that CD-B is capable of assembling with GOX to form stable nanohybrids through non-covalent interactions. As a control, GOX@CDs was prepared in a similar way. The GOX content in GOX@CD-B and GOX@CDs were calculated as 62.3% and 2.0%, respectively, according to the BSA standard curve (Fig. S3), determined by the BCA protein concentration quantification kit. Moreover, the water solubility of GOX@CD-B is higher than that of GOX@CDs (Fig. S4a).

The enzymatic activity of GOX in GOX@CD-B was evaluated by using a cascade of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and O-dianisidine. The absorption changes of O-dianisidine at 460 nm was monitored by UV–vis spectrometry and the kinetic curve was plotted (Fig. 4d). The catalytic activity of GOX@CD-B is 93.8%, demonstrating that GOX@CD-B can well retain the activity of GOX (Fig. S4b). The stability of GOX was evaluated by measuring changes in enzyme activity after 4 d of storage. As shown in Fig. 4e, only 24% of enzyme activity decrease was observed for GOX@CD-B. However, the catalytic activity of free GOX was reduced by 30%. These results demonstrate that the combination of GOX and CD-B can enhance the stability of GOX. In addition, SDS-PAGE test was carried out to confirm the successful construction of GOX@CD-B nanohybrid. As shown in Fig. 4f, free GOX is faster than GOX@CD-B, suggesting that GOX is definitely incorporated into CD-B with enough stability in electric fields. Next, we evaluated the ability of GOX@CD-B to catalyze glucose to produce H2O2 in vitro, according to the steps described in the experimental part (Fig. 4g). A linear standard curve was determined based on the relationship between different concentrations of H2O2 and the absorbance at 407 nm (Fig. 4h). In Fig. S5a, b and Fig. 4i, the content of H2O2 produced by GOX and GOX@CD-B at different time was monitored by UV–vis spectrometry, the results proved that CD-B can effectively retain the ability of GOX to catalyze glucose to produce H2O2.

The cellular uptake capacity of GOX@CD-Bwas evaluated by CLSM. HeLa cells were incubated with varied concentrations of CD-B@GOX for 30 min and then subjected to CLSM. As shown in Fig. S6, HeLa cells can emit green and red light under the excitation of 488 nm and 555 nm, respectively. Moreover, the fluorescence of HeLa cells increases gradually with the concentration increasing from 0.25 μg/mL to 1.0 μg/mL, implying that the intracellular uptake of CD-B@GOX was in a time-dependentmanner.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of CD-B, GOX and GOX@CD-B against HeLa cells were investigated using the standard methyl thiazoletetrazolium (MTT) assay. HeLa cells were cultured with different concentrations of CD-B, GOX and GOX@CD-B. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the survival rates of HeLa cells are all over 90% after incubated with different concentrations of CD-B for 24 h, which verify that CD-B has good biocompatibility and could act as a promising nanocarrier for protein delivery. However, the survival rates of cells treated with GOX@CD-B are comparative with that of GOX-treated cells, only 16% of cells are survival when incubated with 1.0 μg/mL of GOX. The results demonstrate that GOX@CD-B can well preserve the enzymatic activity of GOX and effectively catalyze the oxidization of glucose into H2O2, to cause a high degree of cell death.

Next, we tested the ability of GOX and GOX@CD-B to generate H2O2 in HeLa cells by using reactive oxygen species detection probe (2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA)). As depicted in Fig. 5b, no fluorescence is observed in the DMEM group, while the cells incubated with GOX and GOX@CD-B emit intense green fluorescence, confirming that both GOX@CD-B can catalyze the production of H2O2 from glucose in cells.

In order to more intuitively evaluate the ability of GOX@CD-B to produce H2O2 in vitro, HeLa cells were stained with calcein-AM and PI to identify live/dead cells. As shown in Fig. 5c, cells treated with CD-B are all alive with strong green fluorescence, suggesting that CD-B has none toxicity toward cells. When incubated with 0.125 μg/mL of GOX or GOX@CD-B, most of cells are apoptotic with red fluorescence and only a few cells survived. While almost all of the cells are dead when incubated with 1 μg/mL of GOX or GOX@CD-B, demonstrating that GOX@CD-B have the same catalytic efficiency with GOX.

CD-B containing boric acid was prepared by the modification of amino-riched CDs with 4-(bromomethyl)phenylboronic acid. CD-B could load Hb and GOX to construct Hb@CD-B and GOX@CD-B nanohybrids successfully through multiple noncovalent interactions. Compared to CDs, CD-B can dramatically improve the loading efficiency of Hb and GOX, with the loading rate of 94.3% and 62.3%, respectively. Hb@CD-B and GOX@CD-B have high water solubility and stability, furthermore, both of them are able to achieve efficient intracellular delivery of proteins and maintain the biological activity of proteins. This kind of phenylboronic acid decorated CDs with high affinity towards proteins might be used for loading various biomacromolecules with potential applications in near future.

Shan Lu: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing - original draft, Data curation. Zhigang Xie: Writing - review & editing. Min Zheng: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Jilin Province Science and Technology Research Project (No. 20200201088JC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ces.2021.116586.

ce:section.ce:para:
